title: Triple Bunk Staggered Beds
summary: A staggered three-tier bunk built almost entirely from 1x4s and bolted together so it knocks down — an ambitious, lumber-heavy build for a shared kids' room.
description:
This is a serious project: three staggered bunk beds stacked and offset so the built-in ladder steps climb through the frame. It's framed from an enormous quantity of 1x4s with 2x3 slat supports, and — crucially — it's bolted rather than glued so it can be disassembled to move through doorways and up stairs. Read the whole plan and stage your cuts before you start; there are well over a hundred pieces.

The method is panel-based. You build flat panels — end pieces, inner cross sections, front/back double sections, and front/back single/ladder sections — each with rails marked at a consistent 12-3/4" spacing down the verticals. The staggered geometry comes from where each panel's rails and steps land, so mark carefully from the top down using the given dimensions; a mismeasured panel throws off every bolt hole that references it.

Two details make it safe and reusable. Bolt holes are drilled about 1" below each bed rail (and below the top safety rail) so the nuts sit in safe positions directly under the cross rails, out of the way of sleepers. And the slats are fastened to each other rather than screwed to the supports, so they hold their spacing as a mat but lift out for disassembly. This is a build to take slow — and with a bunk this tall, the top safety rails are non-negotiable.

tools.note: Well over a hundred repeat crosscuts — a stop block saves your sanity.
For the bolt holes through the verticals.

materials.name: 1x4 board, 8 ft
materials.note: The bulk of the frame, rails, steps, and slats.

materials.name: 3" bolts with nuts and washers
materials.note: Bolt holes drilled ~1" below each rail so nuts sit safely under the cross rails.

steps.title: Build the end panels
steps.body: Build the two end pieces — one full height, one for the mid-bunk end. On the verticals, mark from the top down at 12-3/4", 25-1/2", and 38-1/4", plus 51" and 63-3/4" on the full-height vertical, with the last mark 7" from the bottom. These marks set every rail height, so measure them precisely.

steps.title: Build the inner cross sections
steps.body: Build the two inner cross sections — one full height, one hanging. Rails sit the same 12-3/4" apart as the ends. Each cross section has one rail installed flat, which becomes a mattress support.

steps.title: Build the front and back double sections
steps.body: Build the front and back double sections as mirror images of each other. Mark the full-height verticals from the top at 12-3/4", 25-1/2", 38-1/4", 51", and 63-3/4" (last mark 7" up), aligning to the end pieces' rails.

steps.title: Build the single/ladder sections
steps.body: Build the front and back single/ladder sections, also mirror images. Mark the verticals at the same spacing as the double sections. On these, the main verticals sit on the outside of the rails so the ladder steps read cleanly.

steps.title: Drill the bolt holes
steps.body: Drill the connection bolt holes about 1" below each bed rail, plus one below the top safety rail. That places the nuts safely directly below the cross rails, away from sleepers — four bolts in each of the three full-height verticals, and so on per the plan.

steps.title: Connect the slats and assemble
steps.body: Rather than screwing slats to the supports (which would kill the knockdown feature), fasten the slats to each other so they hold spacing as a liftable mat. Then bolt the panels together into the staggered frame. Do not omit the top safety rails — this bunk is tall.

images.alt: Staggered triple bunk bed built from 1x4 lumber
